text: Static stretching immediately before exercise causes significant reductions in maximal voluntary strength, muscle power, and evoked contractile properties.
Dynamic stretching increases range of motion around a joint and enhances subsequent muscular performance including force, power, sprint, and jump outcomes.
The increased range of motion from dynamic stretching is mainly attributable to reduced stiffness of the muscle-tendon unit.
The performance-enhancing effect of dynamic stretching is driven by temperature increases and post-activation potentiation mechanisms from the voluntary contractions involved.
Dynamic stretching is a suitable alternative to static stretching during warm-up when the goal is to increase joint range of motion and enhance muscle force and/or power.
Ballistic stretching (dynamic stretching performed at greater velocities) is less beneficial than controlled dynamic stretching and may impair performance.
The effects of dynamic stretching on performance are modulated by stretch duration, amplitude, and velocity, with some protocols producing no benefit or even performance impairment.
